ops.title: Async tools/call dispatch with WaitGroup synchronization
ops.body:
# Async tools/call dispatch with WaitGroup synchronization

Since the Phase-4 channel change, `tools/call` dispatch runs one goroutine per call; everything else (initialize, tools/list, notifications, error paths) stays inline in the Serve read loop. Constraints that must survive any future edit here:

- **Add-before-spawn, Wait-after-loop.** `s.work.Add(1)` runs on the Serve goroutine before each handler goroutine spawns; `Serve` defers `s.work.Wait()`. Add and Wait are therefore never concurrent (no WaitGroup misuse), and no handler goroutine can write to the output writer after Serve returns.
- **Why WaitGroup, not a closed flag:** the package documents — and `TestServerNotify` asserts — that `Notify` keeps working after `Serve` returns (the writer stays bound). A closed flag under the write lock would break that contract. A blocked handler holding up `Serve`'s return is the accepted trade: handlers receive Serve's ctx, so the wait is bounded by handler responsiveness to cancellation.
- **Write serialization:** the mutex'd `write` holds the lock across the whole frame, so concurrent replies/notifications can't interleave bytes. Never split a frame across two `write` calls.
- **Reply order is NOT request order** anymore. Tests must match replies by JSON-RPC id (`replyByID` helper), never by position — and should assert every request eventually got a reply, so id-matching can't mask a dropped one.
- **Progress:** `Tool.Handler` is `func(ctx, args json.RawMessage, progress func(string)) (string, bool)`. `progress` is never nil — a no-op func when the request carried no `_meta.progressToken`. The token is kept as `json.RawMessage` so string and numeric tokens round-trip byte-identical into `notifications/progress`; the progress counter is a closure-local per-call atomic.
- **RawMessage copies, not aliases:** dispatch copies id/params out of the scanner buffer before handing to a goroutine — the scanner reuses its buffer, so aliasing is a data race.

The park-under-load test (`TestServerParkUnderLoad`) is the guard: a blocked handler must not prevent a sibling tools/call reply, a `Notify` push, or its own progress frames — asserted while the call is still parked. Shipped in commit "channel: async tools/call dispatch and handler progress support"; refuter-verified race-clean (`-race`, 3x suite + 10x targeted).
ops.when: when modifying tools/call dispatch, handler blocking behavior, or the Serve loop's write serialization
